verses.text: Then there was a famine in the days of David three years, year after year; and David enquired of the LORD. And the LORD answered, It is for Saul, and for his bloody house, because he slew the Gibeonites. Guidance: The chapter jumps forward in time — probably ten or more years into the post-rebellion period. A famine grips Israel for three successive years. David, after the third year (Israelite agriculture could often endure one bad year but not three consecutive), inquires of the LORD. The verb daresh — to seek out — is the formal oracle-consultation through the priestly ephod. The LORD answers with an unexpected diagnosis: 'It is for Saul, and for his bloody house, because he slew the Gibeonites.' The famine is not for any of David's sins. It is for a sin of Saul's, decades earlier, that has never been atoned for. The Gibeonites had been an Israelite vassal-population since Joshua 9, when they tricked Israel into a covenant of peace. Saul, at some point during his reign (the event is not narrated in 1 Samuel), 'in his zeal to the children of Israel and Judah' had attempted to exterminate them — breaking the four-hundred-year-old covenant Joshua had sworn in the LORD's name. The LORD's covenant-faithfulness extends across centuries and across regimes: a broken covenant under a former king becomes a famine under his successor. The chapter is teaching us that national sins outlive the kings who commit them. The LORD's books are not closed by a change of reign; unrepented covenant-violations accumulate.

And the king called the Gibeonites, and said unto them; (now the Gibeonites were not of the children of Israel, but of the remnant of the Amorites; and the children of Israel had sworn unto them: and Saul sought to slay them in his zeal to the children of Israel and Judah.) Wherefore David said unto the Gibeonites, What shall I do for you? and wherewith shall I make the atonement, that ye may bless the inheritance of the LORD? And the Gibeonites said unto him, We will have no silver nor gold of Saul, nor of his house; neither for us shalt thou kill any man in Israel. And he said, What ye shall say, that will I do for you. Guidance: David calls the Gibeonites for negotiation. The parenthetical history reminds the reader: the Gibeonites are not Israelite ethnically (they are Amorite/Hivite remnants); they hold their place by the Joshua-era covenant of peace; Saul violated the covenant out of misguided nationalist zeal. David's framing: 'What shall I do for you? and wherewith shall I make the atonement, that ye may bless the inheritance of the LORD?' He is treating the famine as a remediable wrong; he asks the wronged party what would restore the relationship. The Hebrew kipper (atonement) is the standard cultic-restoration verb — David is treating Saul's act as a sin against the LORD that requires the human aggrieved party's input to settle. The Gibeonites refuse a monetary settlement: 'We will have no silver nor gold of Saul, nor of his house.' Blood-debt cannot be paid in cash. They also reject indiscriminate vengeance: 'Neither for us shalt thou kill any man in Israel.' They will not ask David to harm general Israelites. They want something specific from Saul's house. David promises whatever they ask. The chapter is showing us a king who treats reparation seriously, lets the victim define the terms, and accepts what the victim names — the model of restorative justice.

And they answered the king, The man that consumed us, and that devised against us that we should be destroyed from remaining in any of the coasts of Israel, Let seven men of his sons be delivered unto us, and we will hang them up unto the LORD in Gibeah of Saul, whom the LORD did choose. And the king said, I will give them. Guidance: The Gibeonite demand. 'The man that consumed us, and that devised against us that we should be destroyed from remaining in any of the coasts of Israel' — Saul, named not by name but by the genocidal intent he had pursued against them. 'Let seven men of his sons be delivered unto us.' Seven male descendants. 'We will hang them up unto the LORD' — public execution as a religious-judicial act, performed in the LORD's name as cultic atonement. 'In Gibeah of Saul' — Saul's hometown; the executions are to be done where the original perpetrator lived. 'Whom the LORD did choose' — the Gibeonites acknowledge Saul's anointing; their grievance is not against the kingship but against the king's specific betrayal of covenant. David agrees. The decision is morally complex and modern readers find it difficult; the Old Testament considers covenant-violation a blood-debt that the LORD himself collects through proportional human means. The famine had been the LORD's response to the unpaid blood-debt; the Gibeonite-requested executions are the form of payment the wronged party demands. The chapter is recording an exercise in restorative justice by ancient covenant standards. The narrator does not editorialize on the morality; he records what was done, why, and what its result was.

But the king spared Mephibosheth, the son of Jonathan the son of Saul, because of the LORD's oath that was between them, between David and Jonathan the son of Saul. But the king took the two sons of Rizpah the daughter of Aiah, whom she bare unto Saul, Armoni and Mephibosheth; and the five sons of Michal the daughter of Saul, whom she brought up for Adriel the son of Barzillai the Meholathite: And he delivered them into the hands of the Gibeonites, and they hanged them in the hill before the LORD: and they fell all seven together, and were put to death in the days of harvest, in the first days, in the beginning of barley harvest. Guidance: The selection. (1) Mephibosheth — Jonathan's son — is explicitly spared, 'because of the LORD's oath that was between them, between David and Jonathan' (1 Sam 20:14-17, 42). David's covenant with Jonathan survives even the Gibeonite atonement; the chesed he showed in chapter 9 remains binding. (2) The seven selected: Armoni and Mephibosheth (another Mephibosheth — confusingly the same name as Jonathan's son), the two sons of Rizpah and Saul; plus the five sons of Merab (the KJV's 'Michal' is a copyist error; 1 Sam 18:19 records Saul's elder daughter Merab being given to Adriel, and the LXX correctly reads 'Merab' here). These are Saulide grandsons through both lines — two of Saul's own sons by his concubine, and five of Saul's grandsons through his daughter. The seven are 'hanged in the hill before the LORD' at Gibeah, in the beginning of barley harvest (April-May). The narrator's detail about timing — 'in the days of harvest, in the first days, in the beginning of barley harvest' — will become relevant for Rizpah's vigil. The Saulide line, except for Mephibosheth (and his descendants), is functionally extinguished. The chapter is recording the most painful execution-event in 2 Samuel, performed not by David's vengeance but by the Gibeonite-requested atonement. The historical Davidic dynasty is being purged of the political competitors that the chapter-9 chesed had merely held in check; the Gibeonite reckoning produces the political consequence the kindness had postponed. There is no purely-good moral verdict to render here. The chapter holds the complexity.

And Rizpah the daughter of Aiah took sackcloth, and spread it for her upon the rock, from the beginning of harvest until water dropped upon them out of heaven, and suffered neither the birds of the air to rest on them by day, nor the beasts of the field by night. Guidance: One of the most heartbreaking verses in the Old Testament. Rizpah — Saul's concubine, the mother of two of the seven executed — refuses to leave her sons' bodies. Israelite execution by hanging often required the bodies to be left exposed for a day as warning (Deut 21:22-23 set the maximum at one day, then required burial); the Gibeonites apparently intended the seven bodies to hang as long as required. Rizpah cannot allow them to be consumed by scavengers. She takes sackcloth (the standard mourning fabric) and spreads it on the rock at the execution-site. She camps there. 'From the beginning of harvest until water dropped upon them out of heaven' — from April to October or later; possibly six months. Through the entire dry season and the early rains. She drives off carrion-birds by day and predators by night. The image is mythic. A mother with a piece of cloth and a stick (the implements are not named but implied) at a hilltop in Gibeah, alone, for half a year, keeping the bodies of her dead intact through scavenger-vigilance. The chapter is recording one of the great vigils of motherhood in biblical literature. Whatever the morality of the executions, Rizpah's care for her dead is one of the highest acts of love in 2 Samuel. Word of her vigil will reach David and prompt the response that follows. Sometimes the most powerful protest against an injustice — or an unbearable verdict accepted in justice — is to honor the dead with a presence the deciders could not be bothered to provide.

And it was told David what Rizpah the daughter of Aiah, the concubine of Saul, had done. And David went and took the bones of Saul and the bones of Jonathan his son from the men of Jabesh-gilead, which had stolen them from the street of Beth-shan, where the Philistines had hanged them, when the Philistines had slain Saul in Gilboa: And he brought up from thence the bones of Saul and the bones of Jonathan his son; and they gathered the bones of them that were hanged. And the bones of Saul and Jonathan his son buried they in the country of Benjamin in Zelah, in the sepulchre of Kish his father: and they performed all that the king commanded. And after that God was intreated for the land. Guidance: Rizpah's vigil moves David to action. He retrieves Saul and Jonathan's bones from Jabesh-gilead — the heroes who had buried them under the tamarisk tree in 1 Sam 31:13 had given them an honorable interim grave, but the bones had never been brought to the family tomb. David collects them, gathers the seven newly-executed bones (Rizpah's vigil has preserved them in identifiable form), and performs a full state burial in 'Zelah, in the sepulchre of Kish' — Saul's father's tomb, in the Benjamite countryside. The whole Saulide royal family is reunited in the ancestral tomb: Saul, Jonathan, the two sons of Rizpah, the five sons of Merab. The honor David denied Saul during his reign — David never gave Saul a public state burial; the Jabesh-gilead crew had buried him quietly — David now provides. The chapter closes its first major movement with the line: 'After that God was intreated for the land.' The Hebrew vayye'ater elohim la-aretz — 'God was entreated for the land' — i.e., the famine ended; the LORD accepted the atonement. The land received rain again. The chapter is showing us that the LORD's books, having been opened by a long-delayed blood-debt, were finally closed by a long-delayed combination of justice (the Gibeonites' demand) and honor (Rizpah's vigil and David's burial). Both were needed. The chapter has not made the executions easier to read; it has made them part of a larger picture of how covenant violations get settled across generations. Israel got rain; Saul's line got a proper grave; the Gibeonites got their reparation. The closure is bitter and incomplete, but it is closure.

Moreover the Philistines had yet war again with Israel; and David went down, and his servants with him, and fought against the Philistines: and David waxed faint. And Ishbi-benob, which was of the sons of the giant, the weight of whose spear weighed three hundred shekels of brass in weight, he being girded with a new sword, thought to have slain David. But Abishai the son of Zeruiah succoured him, and smote the Philistine, and killed him. Then the men of David sware unto him, saying, Thou shalt go no more out with us to battle, that thou quench not the light of Israel. Guidance: The chapter pivots to a series of giant-fights. The Philistine wars resume. David — now in his sixties — goes out with his army, but 'waxed faint' (the Hebrew suggests battle-fatigue, the body of an aging warrior). Ishbi-benob, 'of the sons of the giant' (a Rephaim — the giant clan around Gath that produced Goliath in 1 Sam 17), targets David. The giant's spear weighs 300 shekels of bronze (about 7.5 pounds, roughly half of Goliath's spear-head per 1 Sam 17:7) and he is girded with a new sword. He sees the king at his most vulnerable — an aging warrior whose strength is failing. Abishai (still the front-line muscle of David's reign) intervenes and kills the giant. The men's response: 'Thou shalt go no more out with us to battle, that thou quench not the light of Israel.' The 'light of Israel' (ner yisrael) is a powerful designation for the king — David is the lamp of God's people; his death would extinguish hope. The army formally restricts the king from combat. The chapter is showing us David accepting limits. The man who fought Goliath as a teenager (1 Sam 17) is now too old to defend himself against Goliath's sons. The kingdom is moving into the period when David's sons must replace his battlefield role. The retirement from front-line combat is the institutional acknowledgment of aging.

And Ishbi-benob, which was of the sons of the giant, the weight of whose spear weighed three hundred shekels of brass in weight, he being girded with a new sword, thought to have slain David. But Abishai the son of Zeruiah succoured him, and smote the Philistine, and killed him. Then the men of David sware unto him, saying, Thou shalt go no more out with us to battle, that thou quench not the light of Israel. Guidance: The chapter pivots to a series of giant-fights. The Philistine wars resume. David — now in his sixties — goes out with his army, but 'waxed faint' (the Hebrew suggests battle-fatigue, the body of an aging warrior). Ishbi-benob, 'of the sons of the giant' (a Rephaim — the giant clan around Gath that produced Goliath in 1 Sam 17), targets David. The giant's spear weighs 300 shekels of bronze (about 7.5 pounds, roughly half of Goliath's spear-head per 1 Sam 17:7) and he is girded with a new sword. He sees the king at his most vulnerable — an aging warrior whose strength is failing. Abishai (still the front-line muscle of David's reign) intervenes and kills the giant. The men's response: 'Thou shalt go no more out with us to battle, that thou quench not the light of Israel.' The 'light of Israel' (ner yisrael) is a powerful designation for the king — David is the lamp of God's people; his death would extinguish hope. The army formally restricts the king from combat. The chapter is showing us David accepting limits. The man who fought Goliath as a teenager (1 Sam 17) is now too old to defend himself against Goliath's sons. The kingdom is moving into the period when David's sons must replace his battlefield role. The retirement from front-line combat is the institutional acknowledgment of aging.

And it came to pass after this, that there was again a battle with the Philistines at Gob: then Sibbechai the Hushathite slew Saph, which was of the sons of the giant. Guidance: A second giant-fight. At Gob (a location west of Jerusalem, exact site unclear) Sibbechai — a member of David's mighty men later listed in 23:27 — kills Saph (also spelled Sippai in 1 Chr 20:4), another son of the Rephaim. The single-verse account names the killer, the place, the giant. The pattern is the chapter's: David's elite warriors are systematically picking off the residual Rephaim warriors of Gath. The 'sons of the giant' — possibly the surviving lineage of Goliath's extended family — are being eliminated one at a time across multiple campaigns. The chapter is showing us that the Goliath story of 1 Sam 17 has a long tail. David killed the famous giant decades earlier; his men are still killing Goliath's relatives. The threat of Rephaim military lineage is being permanently broken over a generation. There is a category of conflict that requires not one heroic moment but a long, methodical clean-up by the heroes that grew up after the original victory.

And there was again a battle in Gob with the Philistines, where Elhanan the son of Jaare-oregim, a Bethlehemite, slew the brother of Goliath the Gittite, the staff of whose spear was like a weaver's beam. Guidance: The third giant-fight. Elhanan son of Jaare-oregim of Bethlehem kills 'the brother of Goliath the Gittite.' Note the KJV's helpful clarification — 'the brother of' is added by the translators following 1 Chr 20:5 which reads 'Lahmi the brother of Goliath.' The Hebrew text of this verse is shorter and ambiguous — it reads 'Elhanan… slew Goliath the Gittite,' which has produced the textual question of whether Elhanan or David killed Goliath. The most likely explanation is a copyist transmission issue: the original probably named Lahmi (Goliath's brother), and 1 Chr preserves the fuller reading. The detail 'the staff of whose spear was like a weaver's beam' is the famous descriptor used of Goliath himself in 1 Sam 17:7, suggesting Lahmi inherited his brother's signature weapon (or used the same equipment-style). Elhanan is identified as 'a Bethlehemite' — David's hometown; the kingdom's heroes are still being recruited from the same villages they always were. The chapter is recording another Rephaim warrior's death. The pattern continues.

And there was yet a battle in Gath, where was a man of great stature, that had on every hand six fingers, and on every foot six toes, four and twenty in number; and he also was born to the giant. And when he defied Israel, Jonathan the son of Shimea the brother of David slew him. These four were born to the giant in Gath, and fell by the hand of David, and by the hand of his servants. Guidance: The fourth and last giant-fight. At Gath itself — Goliath's hometown — Israel faces a giant with polydactyly: six fingers on each hand, six toes on each foot, twenty-four digits total. The detail is anatomically realistic (polydactyly is a real condition; six-fingered families have been documented in Gaza-area archaeology) and theologically pointed (the man's extra fingers may have given him grip-strength advantages with weapons). 'He also was born to the giant' — the Rephaim line again. He 'defied Israel' (charaph — the same verb used of Goliath in 1 Sam 17:10, 17:25, 17:36) — the same kind of public taunt-challenge his ancestor used. Jonathan son of Shimea — David's nephew, the brother of Jonadab from chapter 13 — kills him. The verse's closing summary: 'These four were born to the giant in Gath, and fell by the hand of David, and by the hand of his servants.' The four-giant arc is closed. Goliath's family — at least four named warriors — has been completely eliminated by David and his men across roughly forty years. The Rephaim threat that defined the early Davidic wars is now extinct. The chapter ends with the picture of an aging king, restricted from combat himself, whose generals and nephews have completed the long task he began as a shepherd boy. There is a category of life-work that does not finish in the lifetime of the worker but is completed by those he trained. David trained his men against giants for decades; in his old age, his men are still finishing the lineage he started killing. The chapter ends on the long-arc theology of faithfulness across generations.
